r_1.text:
The back channel is narrow — barely wide enough for the Adamant's beam — and dark. No moon tonight. You navigate by memory and the phosphorescence that traces the reef edges in pale green fire. Behind you, the open sea. Ahead, Nassau.

The blockade has changed. Four ships now, not three. Ashford has brought a fourth vessel — a brig, lighter than the others, positioned to cover the shallows where the larger ships cannot go. The line stretches across the entire harbour mouth. No gaps. No running through.

But the back channel was never on Ashford's charts. The reef closes overhead in places, and twice the Adamant's keel scrapes coral with a sound that makes your teeth ache. Pell, at the bow with a sounding pole, calls depth in a whisper. Six feet. Five. Six again.

Then the channel opens, and you are inside the harbour. Nassau lies before you — lanterns along the waterfront, the fort dark on the hill above, the smell of woodsmoke and low tide.

You have returned. What you brought back will determine what happens next.

choices.text: Head through the waterfront to the fort
Take stock of what you carry

r_2.text:
You stand in the Adamant's cabin and lay out what you have. The lantern swings with the harbour swell, throwing shadows across the table.

The crew is quiet on deck. They know what is coming — everyone in Nassau knows. Ashford's patience has limits, and eleven days became fourteen became twenty-one. The town is waiting for the end of something.

choices.text: Prepare for the rally — the letters will break Ashford
Prepare for the rally — the gold will buy Nassau's defence
Seek out Morrow — she chose to stand with you
Seek out Morrow — she owes you a debt
Send word to Ashford's flagship
You have nothing. Walk into Nassau and see what is left.

r_3.text:
You find Morrow at the Broken Wheel. She is alone — the taproom empty at this hour, chairs stacked on tables, a single candle on the bar. She is reading a letter. She folds it when she sees you and puts it in her coat.

"You came back," she says. Not a question. She looks at you the way someone looks at a thing they have been waiting for without admitting it. "I was not certain you would."

She listens as you tell her what you found at the San Cristobal. What you brought back. She does not interrupt. When you finish, she is quiet for a long time.

"Ashford added a fourth ship three days ago," she says. "The brig Perseverance. He is closing the net. If Nassau does not act in the next two days, he will come ashore with marines and take what he wants."

She stands. "You are going to call the captains together. I know this because there is no other move left. And I am going to stand beside you when you do it."

She reaches into her coat and takes out a heavy brass seal — her personal seal, the one that carries her name and reputation in every port from here to Tortuga.

"Take it," she says. "Show it to the captains. Let them see that Reyna Morrow stands with you. Not because she was forced. Because she chose to."

choices.text: Take the seal and prepare the rally

r_4.text:
You find Morrow at the fort. She is standing on the lower wall, looking out at the blockade. Four ships now, their riding lights steady in the dark.

She does not turn when you approach. "I heard the Adamant came through the back channel," she says. "Half the harbour heard your keel on the coral."

You tell her what you found. What you brought back. She listens without expression.

"You are going to call the captains together," she says. It is not a question. "And you expect me to stand there."

"I do."

A long silence. The blockade lights do not move.

"I will be there," she says finally. "I will stand behind you. I will not speak. You used what you knew about me to put me in this position, and I have accepted that. But do not ask me to pretend it was a kindness."

She walks past you toward the stairs. She does not offer her seal.

choices.text: Accept her terms and prepare the rally

r_5.text:
The word goes out before dawn. By lantern, by runner, by the particular network of rumour that moves through Nassau faster than any signal flag.

A meeting. The fort's lower walls. Every captain in harbour.

You spend the morning preparing. The fort's lower battery has a wide stone platform that overlooks the harbour — you can see the blockade from here, four ships in a line, close enough to read their colours. It is the right place. The captains will see what they are deciding about.

By noon, they begin to arrive. You count them as they come through the gate — eleven captains, plus their mates and trusted hands. Perhaps sixty people in all, standing on the battery platform, looking at you and at the harbour and at each other.

Eleven captains. Nassau used to have thirty.

The sun is high. The blockade ships sit motionless on flat water. Somewhere aboard the Crown's Justice, Ashford is watching through a glass.

It is time to speak.

choices.text: Step forward and address the captains

r_6.text:
You step to the edge of the platform. The stone is warm under your boots. Below, the harbour. Beyond, the blockade.

"Captains," you say. "You know why you are here. You can see the reason from where you stand — four ships across the harbour mouth. Commodore Ashford has been patient. He will not be patient much longer."

The captains watch you. Some with arms folded. Some leaning on the wall. One — Captain Delacroix, who runs a fast sloop called the Marlin — is shaking his head slowly, already decided.

"Nassau has been free for six years," you continue. "Not because anyone gave us permission. Because we took the risk and held the line. Every one of you has buried crew. Every one of you has turned down a pardon and a pension because you decided this was worth more."

You pause. The wind picks up from the harbour, carrying salt and tar.

"I went to the Shallows of Perdition. I found the San Cristobal. And I brought back what was inside her."

The captains stir. The San Cristobal is a name everyone in Nassau knows.

choices.text: Show the Red Correspondence
Show the war chest
Present Morrow's seal to the captains
Call on the Harbour Council's support
You have nothing to show — make your case with words alone

r_7.text:
You take out the seal. Heavy brass, warm from your pocket. You hold it up so the captains can see it — Morrow's mark, her name, her reputation stamped in metal.

"Captain Reyna Morrow stands with me," you say. "Not under duress. Not for payment. She has given me her seal because she believes Nassau is worth defending."

The captains look at the seal. Then they look at Morrow.

She is standing behind you, on the platform. Visible. Present. Captain Delacroix — the one who was already shaking his head — stops shaking it. Morrow's name carries weight in this harbour. Her seal carries more.

A wavering captain, an older man named Oakes who commands a brigantine called the Patience, steps forward. "If Morrow is in," he says slowly, "then I want to hear the rest."

The crowd shifts. Not convinced — not yet. But listening.

choices.text: Now show the Red Correspondence
Now show the war chest
Call on the Harbour Council's support
Make your case with words

r_8.text:
You hold up the harbour bond — the Council's signed letter, heavy paper, three merchant signatures in ink that is barely dry.

"The Harbour Council stands with Nassau," you say. "Their resources. Their ships. Their provisions. This is not talk — this is a signed commitment. Ships refitted, crews paid, powder and shot provided. The merchants of Nassau are putting their money where their mouth is."

You can see the effect. These captains know what the Council's backing means — not speeches, but supplies. Powder. Sailcloth. Pay for the crews who have been eating their seed corn for weeks.

Captain Villiers, who runs a heavy merchantman pressed into service, nods slowly. "The Council's money is good," she says. "That changes the arithmetic."

choices.text: Now show the Red Correspondence
Now show the war chest
Present Morrow's seal
Call for a vote

r_10.text:
You take out the brass tube. The red wax seal is still intact — you crack it now, deliberately, in front of every captain on the platform. The sound is small but every eye follows your hands.

You unfold the first letter. The paper is stiff with salt but legible. You read it aloud.

"From Commodore James Ashford, HMS Crown's Justice, to His Excellency the Governor of Jamaica. Dated the fourteenth of March, seventeen-fourteen."

The captains are still.

"'I have this day taken the Spanish vessel San Cristobal as a prize of war. Her cargo — fifty thousand pounds sterling in Crown gold bound for the Jamaica garrison — has been secured aboard my flagship. Her crew of two hundred and fourteen men were put to the boats in heavy weather, as the prize crew required could not be spared from my complement.'"

You look up from the letter. "Put to the boats in heavy weather. Two hundred and fourteen men. The San Cristobal sank in the Shallows of Perdition with no crew aboard because Ashford took the gold and cast them off to die."

Silence on the platform. Then Captain Delacroix says, very quietly: "He murdered them for prize money."

"He murdered them for prize money," you confirm. "And he has spent ten years making sure no one found out. That is why he is here. Not for Nassau. For the San Cristobal. For these letters."

choices.text: Let the captains respond

r_11.text:
The response comes like a wave breaking. Not shouting — something quieter and more dangerous. The captains talk among themselves in low voices. You hear Ashford's name repeated. You hear the number — two hundred and fourteen — spoken like a verdict.

Delacroix steps to the wall and looks out at the blockade. "That man sits out there flying the King's colours and he drowned two hundred men for their cargo," he says. His voice carries. "And he is here to blockade us?"

Oakes, the older captain, spits over the wall. "If those letters reach London, Ashford hangs. He knows it. We know it. That changes everything."

The mood on the platform has shifted. These are not men being asked to fight a hopeless battle against the Crown. These are men who have just learned that the Crown's representative is a murderer protecting his own skin.

You feel the rally turning.

choices.text: Press the advantage — Morrow is ready to speak
Press the advantage — Morrow stands behind you
Press the advantage — call on Morrow to speak
Press the advantage — call for unity alone

r_12.text:
Before you can speak, Morrow steps forward.

You did not expect it. She was standing behind you — present, visible, but you had not asked her to speak. She chose this.

She walks to the edge of the platform. The captains watch her. Everyone in Nassau knows Reyna Morrow. Everyone knows what her name means — ten years on this coast, a reputation built on competence and silence and the particular kind of authority that does not need to raise its voice.

She looks out at the blockade. Then she turns to the captains.

"I have sailed these waters for longer than most of you have been captains," she says. Her voice is steady. Not loud. She does not need loud. "I have watched Nassau grow from a camp to a town to something that might, if we do not destroy it ourselves, become a republic that lasts."

She pauses. The wind moves her coat.

"I have made mistakes. I have kept secrets that cost people. I have done things I am not proud of." Her eyes find yours for a moment. Something passes between you — acknowledgment, maybe. Gratitude. Something that has no word.

"But I am here," she continues. "I am standing here because the captain beside me gave me a choice instead of an ultimatum. And I chose Nassau. Not because it is easy. Because it is mine. Because it is ours."

She turns back to the captains. "Ashford is a murderer and a thief hiding behind the King's flag. The letters prove it. The captain beside me risked everything to bring them back. I am asking you — not telling you, asking — to stand with us. Not for me. For the harbour you built. For the lives you chose."

She steps back. The silence that follows is not the silence of hesitation. It is the silence of men making up their minds.

Delacroix is the first. "The Marlin stands with Nassau."

Then Oakes. "The Patience."

One by one. Eleven captains. Eleven ships.

Nassau stands.

choices.text: The rally has succeeded — now deal with Ashford

r_13.text:
You call for unity. Your voice carries across the platform, steady and clear.

"Every one of you has a choice," you say. "You can sail out of Nassau tonight through the back channel, one at a time, and take your chances alone. Or you can stand together and face Ashford with something he cannot break — a harbour full of captains who refuse to kneel."

Morrow is behind you. She does not speak. She stands with her arms at her sides, looking out at the harbour. Her presence says what her silence will not — that she is here, committed, whatever her reasons.

The captains see her. They know what it means.

Delacroix looks at Morrow, then at you. "If Morrow is in, I am in. The Marlin stands."

Oakes follows. Then Villiers. Then the rest, one by one, some reluctantly, some with something like relief.

Eleven ships. Not a fleet. Not enough, maybe. But a line that will hold.

choices.text: The rally has succeeded — now deal with Ashford

r_14.text:
You call for unity, and Morrow steps forward.

Her speech is brief. Professional. She does not waste words.

"The captain speaks the truth," she says. "I have verified the letters myself. Ashford took the San Cristobal's gold and drowned her crew. He is here for the evidence, not for Nassau." She pauses. "I am standing with this captain. I recommend you do the same."

It is not a rousing speech. It is not meant to be. It is Reyna Morrow putting her name on the line, and in Nassau, that means something.

Delacroix nods. "Good enough for me."

The captains commit, one by one. Not all of them are enthusiastic. But they commit.

choices.text: The rally has succeeded — now deal with Ashford

r_15.text:
You have crew bring the chest up from the Adamant. It takes four men to carry it. They set it down on the stone platform with a sound that resonates through the fort.

You kneel and open the lid.

Crown gold. Minted coins packed tight in oiled leather bags, each bag stamped with the Jamaica garrison's mark. The gold catches the noon sun and throws light across the faces of the captains.

Fifty thousand pounds sterling. More money than most of these men have seen in their lives. More money than Nassau's entire economy turns over in a year.

The captains stare.

"This was on the San Cristobal," you say. "Crown gold, bound for Jamaica. Ashford claimed it as prize money ten years ago and lost it when the ship sank. He has been trying to recover it ever since."

You stand. "This gold can fund Nassau's defence. Ships provisioned, crews paid, powder and shot purchased. Or it can buy Ashford off — his price has always been money, and this is enough to make him leave."

The captains look at the gold. They look at you.

choices.text: Use the gold to fund Nassau's defence
Use the gold to buy Ashford off

r_16.text:
"This gold funds Nassau," you say. "Not one captain's purse. Not Ashford's retirement. Nassau's defence. Ships careened and armed. Crews paid a fair wage. Powder and shot enough to make that blockade think twice."

The effect is immediate. Captain Villiers — the practical one, the one who runs numbers — steps forward and looks into the chest. "That is enough to refit every ship in harbour and keep them crewed for three months," she says. "More than enough."

The captains begin to talk. Not about whether to fight — about how. The gold has turned a question of courage into a question of logistics, and logistics they understand.

Delacroix is already sketching approaches on the stone wall with a piece of chalk. Oakes is counting ships on his fingers.

choices.text: Show the Red Correspondence as well
Call for a final vote

r_17.text:
"Ashford's price is money," you say. "It has always been money. This is the gold he lost ten years ago. If we give it back, he has no reason to stay."

The captains are quiet. This is not the speech they expected. Some of them look relieved. Some look disgusted.

Delacroix shakes his head. "You want to pay the man who has been strangling our harbour for three weeks?"

"I want him gone," you say. "The method matters less than the result."

Oakes chews his lip. "It is not brave," he says. "But it might be smart."

The captains do not rally. Not truly. But they do not oppose the plan either. There is a pragmatic, uneasy consensus that if the gold removes Ashford without bloodshed, that is a result worth taking.

You do not set the flag that says Nassau rose together. Because it did not. It survived by paying a bribe, and everyone in the harbour knows it.

choices.text: Prepare to deliver the gold to Ashford
Send word to Ashford's ship under a white flag

r_18.text:
The vote is not formal — it never is in Nassau. It is a matter of voices and nods and the particular silence that means assent.

Delacroix commits the Marlin. Oakes commits the Patience. Villiers commits her merchantman, which she has been quietly arming for a week.

One by one, the captains step forward. The gold made the difference — not inspiration, but calculation. Nassau can fight because Nassau can afford to fight.

Eleven ships. Armed, provisioned, crewed. Not a navy. But not nothing.

Nassau has rallied. It did it for the money, but it did it.

choices.text: Now deal with Ashford

r_20.text:
You have nothing to show but your words and your record and whatever the captains see when they look at you.

"Nassau is free because men like you chose to make it free," you say. "Ashford's blockade will break this harbour. Not today — maybe not this week. But soon. And when it breaks, every one of you sails alone."

You look at them — eleven captains, arms folded, sun-dark faces giving nothing away.

"Or you stand together. Here. Now. Eleven ships is not a fleet, but it is not nothing. It is enough to make Ashford calculate. And a man who is calculating is a man who might negotiate."

The silence stretches. The blockade ships sit on flat water, unchanging.

choices.text: Wait for their answer

r_21.text: The captains look at each other. Without the Red Correspondence, without gold, without Morrow's name — you are asking them to risk their ships on your word alone.

test_luck.lucky_text: Something turns. Maybe it is the way you said it. Maybe it is the blockade, visible and oppressive, reminding every captain what inaction costs. Delacroix uncrosses his arms. "Damn it," he says. "I did not come to Nassau to be careful." He steps forward. Oakes follows. Not all of them — you lose three captains who shake their heads and walk away. But eight ships commit. It is enough.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The silence holds. Then Delacroix shakes his head. "I have a crew to think about," he says. "And you have brought us words." He turns and walks away. Oakes follows him. One by one, the captains disperse. By evening, two of them have slipped out through the back channel. The rally is over. It failed.

r_22.text:
You call for unity. Without Morrow present, without her name, the words carry only what you put into them.

The captains listen. Some nod. Most do not. Without Morrow, without the Council, without anything beyond your voice and the sight of the blockade, the rally is precarious.

choices.text: Show the harbour bond
Press on with words alone

r_23.text:
Eight ships. Not eleven — three captains walked away. But eight is enough. You can feel it in the way the remaining captains stand — straighter, closer together, something shared between them now.

Nassau has rallied. Barely. But it has rallied.

choices.text: Now deal with Ashford

r_24.text:
The fort is empty by afternoon. The captains have returned to their ships. Some are preparing to run the blockade. Some are preparing to surrender.

Nassau did not rally. The republic is dying, and everyone knows it.

You stand on the fort wall alone and watch the blockade ships. Four dark shapes on bright water. Ashford is waiting. He can afford to wait.

But you are still here. You still have choices.

choices.text: Send word to Ashford under a white flag
Attempt to board Ashford's flagship alone
Accept Ashford's terms — hand Nassau over
Sail the Adamant out through the back channel

r_25.text:
Nassau has rallied. The captains are committed. But Ashford is still out there — four ships across the harbour mouth, and the question of how to remove them remains.

You stand on the fort wall with the harbour spread below you. The Adamant is at anchor, her crew making ready. Around her, the other committed ships are doing the same — loading powder, running out guns, lashing down anything that might shift in a fight.

The sun is lowering. By tomorrow, Ashford will know the rally happened. His response will come quickly.

You must act before he does.

choices.text: Use the Red Correspondence against Ashford publicly
Send the Red Correspondence to Ashford privately
Offer Ashford the war chest to leave
Launch a direct assault on the blockade
Attempt to negotiate under a white flag

r_26.text:
The Red Correspondence has already been read at the rally. The captains know. But captains are not enough.

You have the letters copied — three scribes working through the night, producing clean versions of every page. By morning, the copies are posted in the harbour square, nailed to the customs house door, distributed to every tavern on the waterfront.

Ashford's name. The date. The prize claim. The two hundred and fourteen men.

Nassau reads. Nassau understands.

By noon, Ashford's signal flags are flying — rapid, urgent communication between his four ships. By evening, the brig Perseverance has weighed anchor and is standing out to sea.

The first crack in the blockade.

By the following morning, the frigate Fervent follows. Ashford's officers are calculating their own futures — if those letters reach London, association with the Commodore becomes a career-ending liability.

Ashford is aboard the Crown's Justice with the Resolve as his only remaining consort. Two ships. The blockade is broken.

choices.text: Watch and wait — let Ashford decide his fate

r_27.text:
You stand on the fort wall for the third morning and watch the Crown's Justice.

She has not moved. Ashford is still aboard. The Resolve stands beside her, loyal or trapped — you cannot tell which.

Then, as the sun clears the eastern headland, you see the signal. A white flag run up the Crown's Justice's mainmast. Not surrender — parley. Ashford wants to talk.

A boat puts out from the warship. A single officer, white flag at the bow, pulling for the harbour.

The message is brief: Commodore Ashford requests a meeting. Aboard the Crown's Justice. Under flag of truce. One hour.

The captains watch from the fort. Morrow — if she is here — watches from the wall.

choices.text: Go to the meeting aboard the Crown's Justice
Refuse — let him come ashore if he wants to talk

r_28.text:
You take the Adamant's launch. Four rowers. No weapons visible, though you keep your cutlass under your coat.

The Crown's Justice is enormous up close — sixty guns, her hull freshly coppered, the smell of tar and discipline. Marines line her rails, watching you come alongside. A rope ladder is dropped.

You climb. The deck is immaculate. Ashford meets you at the quarterdeck rail.

He is older than you expected. Grey at the temples, clean-shaven, a face that has spent thirty years squinting into wind and sun. His uniform is spotless. His eyes are not — they are red-rimmed, tired. He has not slept well.

"Captain," he says. His voice is precise. Naval. The voice of a man who gives orders and expects them followed. "You have made your point. The letters are public. My officers are drawing their own conclusions."

He turns and walks to the stern gallery. You follow. Through the glass, Nassau is visible — small, defiant, smoke rising from the waterfront.

"I will withdraw," he says. "Not because you have beaten me. Because the letters make my position untenable. My career is finished regardless — I knew that the moment you read them aloud. But I will not compound my crimes by firing on a harbour full of ships that have done nothing more than refuse a blockade."

He faces you. "I have one condition. The original letters. The brass tube. I want it back. Not the copies — I know those are already scattered. The originals. For whatever good it does me."

He waits.

choices.text: Give him the originals — he is finished regardless
Refuse — the originals stay in Nassau

r_28a.text:
You take out the brass tube. The red wax is cracked where you broke it at the rally. You hand it across the table.

Ashford takes it. He holds it for a moment, turning it in his hands. Then he sets it down and looks at you.

"Thank you," he says. The words sound foreign in his mouth — a man not accustomed to thanking anyone. "For what it is worth, the men on the San Cristobal — I have thought about them every day for ten years. It does not make it better. But I have thought about them."

He stands. "My ships will clear the harbour mouth by sunset. You will not see me again."

He offers his hand. You take it.

You climb back down to your launch. Behind you, the Crown's Justice begins to make sail.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_28b.text:
"No," you say. "The originals stay in Nassau. They are Nassau's insurance — proof that the man who blockaded us was a murderer. If anyone from the Crown comes again, they will know what was done here."

Ashford's jaw tightens. For a moment you see the officer he was — hard, certain, accustomed to command. Then it passes.

"So be it," he says. "My ships will clear the harbour mouth by sunset. What remains of my reputation is already in your hands. I suppose keeping the paper hardly matters."

He turns away. The meeting is over.

You climb back down to your launch.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_29.text:
You send the boat back with a message: if Commodore Ashford wishes to discuss terms, he may come ashore. Nassau does not go to him.

The captains approve. It is a small thing — pride, positioning — but it matters.

Ashford comes. He is rowed ashore in the Crown's Justice's captain's gig, flanked by two marines who stand rigidly in the boat and do not disembark. He steps onto the harbour stones in his best uniform, white wig, sword at his side.

He looks at Nassau — the fort, the ships, the captains watching from the walls — and you can see him calculating.

"Captain," he says. "You have made your point. The letters are public. I am prepared to withdraw."

The conversation is brief. He asks for the originals. You give them or you do not. It hardly matters — the copies are already in every tavern.

His ships leave by sunset. The blockade is over.

Nassau watches them go.

choices.text: Watch from the fort wall as the ships depart

r_30.text:
You do not use the rally. You do not make the letters public. Instead, you use the back channel — Ashford's contact, the careful communication that has been open since Nassau.

A sealed copy of the first letter goes to the Crown's Justice by boat. No message. No demands. Just the letter, in a sealed envelope, addressed to Commodore Ashford.

You wait on the fort wall. The captains are still committed — they do not know about the private approach. Below, the harbour is tense.

Within the hour, a signal flag runs up the Crown's Justice's mainmast. Three flags, read left to right: a request for negotiation.

Then the Perseverance weighs anchor and begins to move. Not toward Nassau — away. Ashford is pulling back his outermost ship.

The message is clear: he will deal.

choices.text: Send your terms — Ashford withdraws completely

r_31.text:
The terms go back by boat. Simple, specific: Ashford withdraws all four ships. Nassau is not blockaded, not raided, not interfered with. In return, the Red Correspondence is not published. The originals and all copies remain in Nassau — insurance, not ammunition.

Ashford's response comes within two hours. A single word, written in his own hand on Crown stationery: *Agreed.*

By evening, the blockade is dissolving. The Perseverance is already over the horizon. The Fervent and Resolve are making sail. The Crown's Justice is last — she turns broadside to the harbour one final time, and you imagine Ashford standing at his stern gallery, looking at Nassau for the last time.

Then she makes sail. The harbour mouth is empty.

The blockade is over. Nassau survives. The secret is kept.

choices.text: Consider what this means

r_32.text:
Nassau is saved. But not cleanly.

The captains will never know how the blockade really ended. They will think their rally did it — their courage, their unity. And it helped. But the real lever was a private letter and a private threat, and Ashford withdrew not because he was outmatched but because he was outmanoeuvred.

The republic survives under a secret. You hold the letters. Ashford knows you hold them. Neither of you can speak of the arrangement.

It is not justice. It is survival.

choices.text: Accept the uneasy peace

r_35.text:
The Dragon's Breath. You brought the sealed canisters up from the San Cristobal — volatile, decades old, dangerous to everyone within a hundred yards. Ship-grade explosive compound, packed in iron casings with waxed fuses.

The plan is Delacroix's, refined by Villiers, executed by Nassau's fastest sloop. Two sloops go first, running for the blockade line at dawn. They draw fire. The Crown's Justice and Resolve turn to engage. While their guns are occupied, the Adamant and three armed merchantmen approach from the back channel — the route you used to enter the harbour.

The Dragon's Breath goes aboard the sloop Greyhound. Her captain, a young man named Torres, volunteers. He knows what the canisters do. He knows the risk.

At dawn, Nassau's ships move.

choices.text: Take the Adamant into battle

r_36.text:
The battle is fast and violent and nothing like the stories.

The two lead sloops draw the Crown's Justice's fire. Cannon smoke rolls across the harbour mouth in a grey wall. The sound is enormous — concussive, physical, a pressure in your chest that makes breathing difficult. Splinters fly from the sloops' rails. One takes a hit below the waterline and begins to list.

Then the Greyhound makes her run.

Torres brings her in from the south, hugging the reef line where the larger ships cannot follow. The Crown's Justice sees her too late. Torres's crew lights the fuses — you can see the sparks from half a mile away, tiny orange points against grey smoke — and the canisters go over the Greyhound's bow rail at close range.

The explosion is not fire. It is pressure. A flat, enormous sound that hits you in the chest like a fist. A column of water goes up beside the Crown's Justice's hull — sixty feet, seventy. When it comes down, it brings pieces of copper sheathing and shattered timber with it.

The Crown's Justice shudders. She does not sink — she is too large, too well-built — but her port side is staved in below the waterline. She begins to list. Her guns fall silent as her crew scrambles to the pumps.

choices.text: Press the attack

r_37.text:
The Resolve tries to cover the Crown's Justice's withdrawal. She puts herself between the crippled flagship and Nassau's ships and fires a broadside that rakes the lead merchantman from stem to stern. Men die. Timber shatters. The merchantman's mainmast comes down in a tangle of rigging.

But Nassau has numbers now. Eight ships — or eleven — against two, and one of those is sinking. The Adamant leads the second wave, coming through the smoke with her guns run out.

You give the order to fire. The Adamant's broadside is small — she is no warship — but at this range, every shot tells. The Resolve takes hits along her waterline. Her captain — you can see him through the smoke, a small figure on the quarterdeck — makes the calculation and strikes his colours.

The Crown's Justice is being towed out to sea by her boats, listing badly. Ashford's flagship is done.

But the cost.

choices.text: Survey the damage — the crew held together
Survey the damage — the crew is scattered

r_38.text:
The Adamant has taken two hits. One through the fo'c'sle — it killed Harker, the young swimmer who dove on the San Cristobal. He was at the bow gun. There was nothing left to bury.

The second hit is in the hull, above the waterline. Repairable. The Adamant will sail again.

Your crew is shaken but intact. Pell is bleeding from a splinter wound across his forehead, but he waves you off when you approach. "It is a scratch, Captain. We won."

The harbour mouth is clear. The Resolve has struck her colours. The Crown's Justice is limping toward the horizon, down by the bow, her pumps working. The Perseverance and Fervent were already gone — fled when the Dragon's Breath went off.

Nassau is free. The cost was real — Harker, the merchantman's crew, the damage — but the Adamant survived. Your crew survived.

You stand on the quarterdeck and look at the harbour you just fought for.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_39.text:
The Adamant is burning.

The second hit — the one that came through the quarterdeck — found the powder store. Not a direct hit, but close enough. The fire started in the magazine passage and spread faster than any man could fight it.

You gave the order to abandon ship. Your crew — what is left of them, the ones who did not scatter during the approach, the ones who stayed at their posts despite everything — go over the side. Pell is the last man off. He looks at you from the water, treading, blood on his face.

"Jump, Captain," he says.

You jump.

The water is warm and tastes of gunpowder. Behind you, the Adamant burns. She is your ship — has been your ship for three years, the only home that matters — and she is gone.

The harbour mouth is clear. The Resolve has struck. The Crown's Justice is crippled. Nassau is free.

The Adamant burns at the harbour mouth, a pyre that lights the water for a hundred yards in every direction. You swim for shore.

Nassau is free. The Adamant is gone.

choices.text: Drag yourself ashore

r_40.text:
The war chest goes to Ashford under a flag of truce. You send it aboard the Crown's Justice in the Adamant's launch — four men rowing, the chest between them, the weight making the boat sit low in the water.

You go with it. You want to see Ashford's face.

The Crown's Justice receives you correctly — naval courtesy, sideboys, a marine escort to the great cabin. Ashford is waiting at his desk. He is writing when you enter and does not look up for a deliberate ten seconds.

"Captain," he says, setting down his pen. "I am told you have brought something."

The chest is carried in. The marines set it down. You open the lid.

The gold catches the lantern light in the dim cabin. Ashford looks at it for a long time. His expression does not change, but his hands — resting on the desk — go very still.

"Fifty thousand pounds," he says. "The Jamaica garrison's payroll. I reported it lost at sea."

"It was lost at sea," you say. "Now it is found. Take it and leave Nassau."

He looks at you. He looks at the gold. He does the calculation — the same calculation he has been doing for ten years, weighing the gold against the secret against the risk.

"Agreed," he says.

His ships leave by sunset.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_41.text:
A white flag goes to the blockade. You send a boat with a simple message: you have the San Cristobal's cargo. You wish to discuss terms.

Ashford sends his gig within the hour. A lieutenant — young, precise, naval to his bones — delivers the response. Commodore Ashford will receive you aboard the Crown's Justice at the Commodore's convenience, which is to say, now.

You go. The war chest goes with you.

The exchange is brief. Ashford sees the gold. He sees the out — a way to recover what he lost without firing a shot, without the risk of the letters surfacing, without the mess of an assault on a harbour that has just rallied.

He takes the deal. His ships leave.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_42.text:
The blockade is over. Ashford's ships are gone — you watch the last of them clear the horizon from the fort wall.

Nassau is quiet in the way that a town is quiet after a storm. People on the waterfront. Ships at anchor. The ordinary sounds of a harbour that has remembered it is a harbour.

The war chest is gone. Ashford took it and left. Nassau is free, bought with gold that was never yours to begin with — Crown money, recovered from a wreck, used to bribe a Crown officer.

No one in Nassau knows how the blockade ended. They think the rally did it. They think their courage — the captains standing together, the ships armed and ready — is what made Ashford withdraw.

Let them think it.

choices.text: Decide what comes next
Return to the Adamant

r_43.text:
Nassau rallied. The captains stood together. And then you bought Ashford off with his own gold, quietly, without telling them.

The result is the same — the blockade is gone. Nassau is free. The captains have their victory. You have the knowledge that it was money, not courage, that ended it.

The Adamant is at anchor, provisioned and ready. Your crew is aboard. The harbour mouth is open for the first time in weeks.

You can stay. Nassau would have you — the captain who rallied them, who brought back the gold, who broke the blockade. Or you can leave. The sea is open. The Adamant is fast.

Either way, you are a free captain.

choices.text: Stay in Nassau — this is your harbour now
Sail out through the harbour mouth — the sea is calling

r_44.text:
The Adamant is ready. Your crew is aboard. The harbour mouth is open.

You paid Ashford off. Nassau did not rally. The republic survives, but not because it stood together — because a chest of Crown gold changed hands.

You can stay, but for what? Nassau will forget you. Or you can take what you have and sail. The Adamant is your ship. The sea is your harbour.

There is nothing keeping you here.

choices.text: Set sail — the deal is done
Set sail — leave Nassau behind

r_50.text:
You use the back channel — Ashford's contact, the careful line of communication that has been open since your first days in Nassau.

A message goes to the Crown's Justice by fishing boat. Nothing written — a verbal message, delivered by a harbour boy who cannot read and does not know what the words mean.

The response comes within two hours. A boat from the Crown's Justice, a lieutenant you have seen before, a sealed note in Ashford's handwriting.

*Come aboard. White flag. Bring what you found.*

Direct and unambiguous. Ashford knows you went to the San Cristobal. He knows you found something. He wants to deal.

choices.text: Go aboard the Crown's Justice
Go aboard — bring the war chest
Go aboard empty-handed — negotiate with nothing

r_51.text:
The Crown's Justice's great cabin. Ashford behind his desk. Marines at the door. The lantern swings with the harbour swell.

You set the brass tube on the desk between you. Ashford looks at it. He knows what it is — he has been waiting ten years for someone to find it.

"The Red Correspondence," you say. "Your letters to Jamaica. The prize claim. The two hundred and fourteen men you put to the boats."

Ashford's face does not change. He is a man who has been expecting this moment for a decade.

"What do you want?" he asks.

You can see the calculation behind his eyes. If those letters reach London, he hangs. Not metaphorically. Actually hangs. A commodore executed for murder and theft — the Admiralty would make an example.

choices.text: Offer the deal — you keep the letters, he leaves Nassau
Demand his unconditional withdrawal
This was a mistake — you should have used the letters publicly

r_52.text:
"You leave," you say. "All four ships. Nassau is not blockaded, not raided, not interfered with. In return, the letters stay in Nassau. Insurance, not ammunition. As long as you leave us alone, they stay sealed."

Ashford considers. You can see him running the numbers — the risk of the letters against the cost of withdrawal against the damage to his career if he returns to London empty-handed.

"And the gold?" he asks. "The war chest?"

"Was never yours," you say. "It belongs to the Jamaica garrison. Or it did. Now it belongs to whoever holds it."

A long silence. The ship creaks around you.

"Agreed," Ashford says. "My ships will clear the harbour mouth by morning."

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_53.text:
"Unconditional," you say. "You withdraw. All four ships. No terms, no conditions, no return. If I see Crown sails off Nassau again, the letters go to London on the next packet ship."

Ashford's jaw works. This is harder for him — not the withdrawal, but the submission. He is a commodore. He gives orders. He does not take them from pirates.

"You are making an enemy," he says quietly.

"You were already an enemy," you say. "Now you are an enemy who is leaving."

Another silence. Longer this time. Then Ashford stands.

"My ships will be gone by sunset," he says. His voice is flat. "Do not contact me again."

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_54.text:
You reach for the brass tube. A mistake — Ashford's hand moves faster.

He does not draw a weapon. He does not need to. He speaks one word — "Marines" — and the door opens and two armed men step inside with muskets levelled.

"You came aboard my ship under a flag of truce," Ashford says. His voice has changed — the careful negotiator is gone. This is the officer. "You brought evidence of a crime that, if made public, ends my career and my life. And now you are reaching for it with an expression that suggests you intend to leave."

He leans forward. "You will not leave."

The marines move. You are disarmed, efficiently, without violence. Your cutlass and flintlock are taken. The brass tube stays on the desk.

Ashford sits down and begins writing. A report, you realise. Your capture. Your crimes. The legal language of piracy — taken in lawful action, subject to Crown justice.

choices.text: There is no escape from this

r_55.text:
You go aboard the Crown's Justice with nothing. No letters. No gold. No leverage.

Ashford receives you correctly — the formalities of a flag of truce observed. He leads you to the great cabin and sits behind his desk.

"Captain," he says. "You are aboard my ship. You have requested this meeting. What do you have to say?"

You have nothing to offer and nothing to threaten. The conversation is short. Ashford listens. He is polite. He is patient. He is immovable.

"Nassau will accept the Crown's terms or Nassau will be taken," he says. "I have four ships and six hundred marines. You have — what? A dozen merchant vessels with popguns? Fishermen with cutlasses?"

He stands. "I will give you until morning to reconsider. After that, I come ashore."

The marines escort you back to the rail.

choices.text: Return to Nassau — you need another plan
Draw your flintlock — one shot

r_56.text:
You return to Nassau empty-handed. The town is quiet. The captains are waiting for news.

You have no news. Ashford will come ashore by morning. Nassau has nothing to stop him.

Unless you can find another path.

choices.text: Use the Red Correspondence publicly
Launch a desperate assault with the Dragon's Breath
Accept that Nassau is lost
Sail the Adamant out — save your ship and crew

r_57.text:
You draw the flintlock.

One shot. You have always had one shot. The pistol has been in your belt since you first stepped aboard the Adamant, cleaned and loaded, the flint sharp, the powder dry. A reliable weapon. One shot, well-placed, ends most arguments before they start.

The marines see the movement. Ashford sees it. Time stretches.

You level the flintlock at Ashford's chest. He does not flinch. He looks at you with the expression of a man who has been shot at before and survived it.

"If you fire," he says, "my marines will kill you. Your ship will be taken. Nassau will burn. One pistol ball does not change the arithmetic."

He is right. You know he is right. One shot changes nothing — except the nature of the ending.

choices.text: Fire — and accept the consequences
Lower the pistol — he is right

r_58.text:
You fire.

The sound is enormous in the closed cabin. Smoke fills the space. The ball hits Ashford in the left shoulder — not the chest, not where you aimed. The ship moved. The shot went wide.

Ashford staggers back, gripping the desk. Blood darkens his uniform. His face goes white, but he does not fall.

"Marines," he says through his teeth.

They are already moving. The cabin door bursts open. Muskets level. You have an empty pistol and a cutlass against six armed men in an enclosed space.

There is no winning this. There was never going to be.

r_58a.text:
You cut down the first marine. The second hesitates — long enough for you to reach the stern gallery. The glass shatters under your boot and you are through it, falling, hitting the water twenty feet below.

The harbour swallows you. Above, musket fire cracks across the water. Balls hit the surface around you in white plumes. You dive deep, lungs burning, swimming blind.

When you surface, gasping, you are fifty yards from the Crown's Justice. The Adamant's launch is waiting — Pell, who saw the muzzle flash from the cabin and brought the boat around.

"Captain," he says, hauling you aboard. "That was either very brave or very stupid."

"Both," you manage.

Ashford is alive. Wounded. Furious. His marines are lining the rail, reloading. But you are out of range, and the launch is pulling for Nassau.

One shot. It did not end the argument. But it changed the terms.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_59.text:
You lower the pistol.

The marines do not relax. Ashford does not blink. He waits until the flintlock is at your side, then nods once.

"A sensible decision," he says. "You may return to your ship. My offer stands — you have until morning."

The marines escort you to the rail. You climb down to the launch with your flintlock still in your belt, unfired. One shot, still loaded. You did not use it.

Was it wisdom or cowardice? Standing in the boat, watching the Crown's Justice recede, you are not certain.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_60.text:
You have nothing. No letters. No gold. No allies.

Nassau is dark and quiet. The waterfront is mostly empty — ships gone, crews scattered, the harbour a shadow of what it was. Ashford's blockade has been strangling the town for weeks and you can see it in the closed shops, the empty taverns, the way people move with their heads down.

You walk the waterfront alone. Your crew is aboard the Adamant, waiting. The town is waiting. Everyone is waiting for something to happen or for nothing to happen, which amounts to the same thing.

Without leverage, without allies, without resources — what can you do?

choices.text: Try to rally the captains anyway
Attempt to negotiate with Ashford directly
Take the Adamant and run — save your ship

r_62.text:
You send word to Ashford. Not terms. Not negotiation. Capitulation.

The message is simple: Nassau will accept the Crown's authority. The harbour is open. In return, you want safe passage for the Adamant and her crew.

Ashford's response comes within the hour. Agreed. The Adamant may leave. Nassau is his.

The captains do not know until they see the Crown's boats coming ashore. By then it is too late. Marines on the waterfront. The Union flag over the fort. Nassau's brief experiment in freedom, over.

You watch from the Adamant's quarterdeck as Nassau becomes a Crown port again. The war chest is in the hold — your passage out, your future, someone else's gold.

The harbour mouth is open. The Adamant makes sail on the evening tide.

choices.text: Sail away — Nassau is behind you

r_63.text:
You take the Adamant out through the back channel at night. The same route in, in reverse — coral scraping the keel, phosphorescence marking the reef edges, Pell calling depth in a whisper.

Nassau falls behind you. The blockade lights recede. The open sea opens.

You did not save Nassau. You did not try hard enough, or you tried and failed, or you decided that saving yourself was more important. The crew does not judge you — they are alive, the ship is whole, the horizon is open.

But something is left behind. Not guilt exactly. Something more like a question that will never be answered — what would have happened if you had stayed?

The wind fills the Adamant's sails. You set a course south. Nassau is someone else's problem now.

choices.text: The sea takes you

r_64.text:
You are moving through the waterfront district in the early morning hours, heading for the fort, when you feel it — a wrongness. Too quiet. The tavern on the corner, which should be serving the night watch, is shuttered and dark. The lantern at the harbour master's post has been extinguished.

You stop. Listen.

Footsteps behind you. More than one pair. Moving carefully on the wet stones.

You turn. Three men in the alley mouth, backlit by the harbour. One of them carries a boarding axe. Another has a club.

The third steps forward. In the dim light, you recognise him — a harbour man, one of the Council's factors. Someone you spoke to. Someone who knew your plans.

"Nothing personal, Captain," he says. "But there is a man on the blockade ships paying good gold for information about you. And better gold for you, personally."

He nods to the other two. They move forward.

choices.text: Fight your way through
Run for the waterfront

r_65.text: You draw your cutlass. The alley is narrow — that helps. They cannot come at you together.

r_66.text:
You run. The wet stones are treacherous — your boots slip once, twice. Behind you, the men give chase.

The waterfront is close. If you can reach the Adamant's dock, your crew will see you. The ship's bell will bring them running.

test_luck.lucky_text: You make it. The dock appears out of the dark, the Adamant's lanterns at the end, and you are running full tilt when Pell sees you from the rail. He shouts. The crew comes alive. The men chasing you see the movement aboard the ship and fall back into the dark. You are safe — but someone in Nassau tried to sell you to Ashford.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Your boot catches a mooring ring in the dark. You go down hard, hands out, chin hitting stone. The world flashes white. When it clears, they are on you. Hands pulling you up. A cloth over your face, smelling of something sweet and chemical. The harbour tilts sideways. The dark closes in.

r_67.text:
You survive the ambush. But the message is clear — someone in Nassau is working for Ashford. The Council's factor, the man who tried to have you taken. Ashford is not just waiting. He is buying.

You return to the Adamant, shaken. Pell has the crew at stations, watching the waterfront.

"We are not safe on shore, Captain," he says. It is not a complaint. It is a fact.

You clean the scrape on your chin and think about what this means. Nassau is not united. Nassau has fractures, and Ashford is finding them.

The rally is more important than ever. Or the whole thing falls apart.

choices.text: Prepare for the rally — there is no more time

r_70.text:
Nassau harbour. The blockade is gone. The harbour mouth stands open — blue water, white caps, the horizon unbroken for the first time in weeks.

The captains are on the waterfront. Some of them are cheering. Some are quiet, watching the empty sea where Ashford's ships were, not quite believing it.

You stand on the fort wall. Below you, the harbour. The Adamant at anchor, her crew on deck, looking up at you. The other ships — eleven of them, or eight, depending on how the rally went — at their moorings, flags flying.

Nassau is free. The question is what kind of freedom.

choices.text: Find Morrow — she stood with you by choice
Find Morrow — she kept her word
Find Morrow — she spoke for Nassau
Address the captains from the fort wall
Return to the Adamant — time to decide what comes next

r_71.text:
Morrow is on the wall. She came up while you were watching the harbour, and she is standing a few paces away, looking out at the sea.

She does not speak immediately. The wind moves her coat. Her hair. The empty harbour mouth where four ships used to be.

"I did not expect to be here," she says finally. Not looking at you. Looking out. "I expected to be running. Or dead. Or somewhere on the other side of the Atlantic, pretending none of this happened."

She turns to you. Her expression is not quite respect and not quite gratitude. It is something more complicated — the face of a person who was given a choice they did not expect and made the right one and is still surprised by it.

"You could have used what you knew about me," she says. "You chose not to. I am standing here because of that choice. I want you to know I understand what it cost you — the leverage you gave up. The risk."

She looks back at the harbour. "Nassau remembers who stood on the wall, Captain. I will make sure of it."

She does not say thank you. She does not need to.

choices.text: Stand together on the wall — Nassau is free
Return to the Adamant

r_72.text:
Morrow is at the Broken Wheel. She is drinking alone. She does not look up when you enter.

"It is over," you say.

She nods. Drinks. Sets the cup down.

"I stood where you told me to stand," she says. "I did what you needed me to do. Nassau is free. Are we finished?"

There is no warmth in it. She did her part because you forced her hand. She kept her word. But she will not pretend it was willing.

"We are finished," you say.

She nods again. "Then I have a ship to see to." She stands, drops a coin on the bar, and walks past you without looking back.

Morrow kept her word. That is all it was — a kept word, nothing more.

choices.text: Return to the fort
Return to the Adamant

r_73.text:
You find Morrow at the harbour. She is watching her own ship — the Celestine — being resupplied. When she sees you, she nods.

"Well done, Captain," she says. Professional. Sincere. Not warm, but genuine. "You did what you said you would do. Nassau is still standing."

She extends her hand. You shake it. The grip is firm and brief.

"If you need me again," she says, "you know where to find me. Nassau captains look out for each other."

She turns back to her ship. The conversation is over. It was enough.

choices.text: Address the captains from the fort wall
Return to the Adamant

r_74.text:
You address the captains from the fort wall. The same platform where the rally was held — the same stone, the same view of the harbour. But the harbour mouth is open now, and the captains are not frightened. They are free.

"Captains," you say. "The blockade is broken. Ashford is gone. Nassau stands."

The cheer that goes up is not organized. It is ragged, spontaneous, loud. Men who have been holding their breath for weeks finally letting it out.

"What happens next is up to you," you continue. "Nassau was always its own republic. It does not need a saviour. It needs captains who stay, who trade, who build. Who remember what this harbour is for."

Delacroix raises a bottle. Oakes claps you on the shoulder. Villiers nods from the back of the crowd — her approval harder to earn and more valuable for it.

Nassau is free. And it knows it.

choices.text: Find Morrow on the wall — she earned this
Look out at the empty harbour mouth
Accept what you have built here

r_75.text:
The Adamant. Your ship. Your crew.

Pell is on the quarterdeck, looking at you. "Orders, Captain?"

The harbour mouth is open. The wind is fair. The Adamant is provisioned and ready to sail. Nassau is behind you — free, chaotic, imperfect, surviving.

You can stay. You can go. The sea does not care which. The sea only cares that you choose.

choices.text: Stay in Nassau — this is home now
Set sail — the horizon is calling
Set sail — there is nothing left for you here
Set sail — leave it all behind

r_76.text:
You stand on the fort wall. Nassau is spread below you — the harbour, the ships, the waterfront coming alive again. Smoke from cook fires. The sound of hammers on hulls as ships are repaired. The ordinary sounds of a town that has survived.

Morrow is beside you. She came up to the wall without being asked, and she stands there — not speaking, not needing to speak. The wind off the harbour smells of salt and tar and something underneath that is not sweet, exactly, but is Nassau.

The Red Correspondence has been read. Ashford is gone. The captains are committed. The Harbour Council's resources are flowing. Everything that could be done right was done right.

This is the ending. Not a perfect one — there is no such thing. But a true one.

choices.text: Nassau remembers who stood on the wall
Nassau remembers

r_77.text:
Nassau is free. Ashford has been exposed. The captains rallied.

But something is missing. Morrow is not beside you — she is in the crowd somewhere, or on her ship, or gone. The victory is real. The cost is real. And the space where a particular person should be standing is empty.

You did what you came to do. Nassau is saved. The question is whether you saved everything that mattered.

choices.text: Accept the victory for what it is

end_saviour_true.text:
Nassau's captains stand on the fort walls and watch the last of Ashford's ships slip below the horizon. The harbour mouth is open — blue water, white light, the sea stretching out in every direction without obstruction. For the first time in weeks, boats are moving freely through the channel. A fishing sloop puts out on the morning tide. A merchant brig begins loading cargo. The ordinary machinery of a free port, turning again.

Morrow is beside you on the wall. She stands with her hands on the stone parapet, looking out at the water. She did not have to be here — you gave her the choice, and she made it, and you have come to understand that this is the difference between a republic that lasts and one that does not. Not the walls. Not the guns. Not even the gold. The choice. The willingness to stand when standing costs something.

Below, in the harbour square, the Red Correspondence has been posted for all to read. Ashford's name is in every sentence — the prize claim, the two hundred and fourteen men, the decade of silence. His ships are gone. His career is over. His crimes are public record now, written in salt-stained ink on the customs house door where every captain in the Caribbean will read them.

The Harbour Council's resources are flowing — ships refitted, crews paid, powder and shot stockpiled in the fort's magazine. The harbour bond you secured has turned words into material reality. Nassau is not just free. Nassau is provisioned, armed, and backed by its own merchants.

Morrow steps back from the wall. She looks at you with something that is not quite respect and not quite gratitude — something more complicated than either, something that has no word in any language you speak. She holds your gaze for a long moment.

"Nassau will remember who stood on the wall," she says. And then, quieter: "So will I."

She walks away. You watch her go — down the fort stairs, across the square, toward the harbour. Her ship is there. Her crew. Her life, which you could have destroyed and chose not to.

You turn back to the sea. The horizon is empty and open and belongs to no one. Nassau is at your back, loud and alive and free. The Adamant is at anchor below, her crew on deck, her sails furled, waiting for your next order.

But for now, you stand on the wall. The sun is warm. The wind smells of salt. And Nassau — imperfect, ungovernable, stubbornly free — is still standing.

Because you stood first.

end_saviour.text:
Nassau is saved.

You stand on the fort wall and watch the harbour come alive again. Ships moving, crews working, the waterfront filling with the noise and commerce of a free port. The blockade is broken. Ashford's name is posted in the harbour square — the Red Correspondence, copied and distributed, nailed to every public surface in the town. His crimes are known. His ships are gone.

The captains rallied. They stood together when it mattered, and that is not nothing — it may be the thing that saves Nassau again, the next time. Because there will be a next time. There is always a next time.

But the wall beside you is empty. Morrow is not here. She is somewhere in the crowd, or aboard her ship, or gone — you do not know which. She helped. She stood where she needed to stand. But the distance between you was never closed, and it will not be closed now.

The victory is real. You earned it. The Red Correspondence broke Ashford's blockade more thoroughly than any broadside could have. The captains know the truth. The harbour knows the truth. And the truth is Nassau's best defense — because the next Crown officer who considers blockading this port will know that Nassau keeps records and Nassau has long memory.

You did what you came to do. Nassau is free. The republic holds.

It is enough. It is nearly enough.

You turn from the wall and walk down to the harbour. The Adamant is waiting. Your crew is waiting. The sea is open, and whatever comes next, you face it as the captain who saved Nassau.

They will remember that. Even if they forget everything else.

end_free_captain.text:
The harbour mouth is open. The wind is fair. The Adamant sits at anchor with her sails ready to drop, and your crew stands at their stations with the particular alertness of men who know they have won something.

Nassau rallied. The captains stood together. The blockade broke — not cleanly, not without cost, but it broke. And now the question is simple: what does a free captain do with freedom?

The war chest is gone — spent on Nassau's defence, or traded for Ashford's withdrawal, or divided among the crews who fought. The gold did its work. It bought time, or loyalty, or passage. You do not regret it. Gold is heavy and freedom is not, and you have always known which matters more.

Pell stands at the helm. He does not ask for orders. He waits. The crew waits. The Adamant waits — she has been waiting for this since you first brought her into Nassau harbour, weeks ago, in another life.

You look at Nassau one last time. The fort on the hill. The harbour below. The waterfront where you walked and talked and fought and made choices that you will carry for the rest of your life. It is a good harbour. It is not your harbour.

Yours is wherever the Adamant takes you.

"Make sail," you say.

The crew moves. Canvas drops and fills. The Adamant turns her bow toward the open sea, and the harbour falls behind you — the fort growing smaller, the ships at anchor shrinking, Nassau becoming a shape on the horizon and then a memory.

You are a free captain on an open sea. Whatever that means — whatever it costs — it is enough.

end_deal.text:
The blockade is gone. Ashford has withdrawn. Nassau survives.

But the survival has a price, and the price is silence.

You stand on the Adamant's quarterdeck and look at the harbour. Ships moving, crews working, the ordinary sounds of a port that has been given a reprieve it does not fully understand. The captains think the rally did it. They think their unity, their courage, their willingness to stand together drove Ashford away.

You know better. The deal was made in private — the Red Correspondence for Ashford's withdrawal. A secret exchanged for a secret. The letters stay in Nassau, sealed, insurance against a return that will never come because Ashford is finished either way. His career is over. His reputation is ash. But it happened quietly, and that is the point.

No one is satisfied. The captains have a victory that is not quite real. You have a peace that is not quite honest. Ashford has his life, which is not quite worth living. And Nassau has its freedom, which is built on a silence that can never be broken.

You told yourself, once, that the truth matters. That the Red Correspondence was important because it was true — because two hundred and fourteen men died and someone should answer for it. No one answered. The truth was used as a lever, not as justice.

Pell brings you coffee. The harbour is quiet. The morning is grey.

"What now, Captain?" he asks.

What now. The question of a man who has survived something and is not sure it was worth surviving. You drink your coffee. You watch the harbour. You keep the secret.

Nassau endures. That will have to be enough.

end_betrayal.text:
The Adamant clears Nassau on a running tide. The back channel swallows you — coral and dark water and the phosphorescence that marks the reef edges like a path through the underworld. Behind you, Nassau's lights dim and disappear.

You stand at the stern rail and watch them go. The fort. The harbour. The waterfront where captains are waking up to discover that the world has changed while they slept — that someone made a deal, or failed to make a stand, or simply left.

The war chest is in the hold. Crown gold, heavy and specific, someone else's fortune funding your escape. You tell yourself that is what the sea asks of a captain — hard choices, clean breaks, the willingness to cut loose when the tide turns against you. It is what every pirate learns or dies learning: nothing is yours. Not the harbour. Not the town. Not the men who trusted you. Everything is borrowed, and the sea collects its debts.

By the time land disappears, you almost believe it.

Pell does not speak. The crew does not speak. They are alive. The ship is whole. The horizon is open and empty and free, and freedom is what you wanted — what you have always wanted — and this is what it looks like.

Nassau is someone else's problem now. The captains will find new leaders or they will scatter or they will surrender. The republic will hold or it will not. It was always fragile — a collection of men who agreed on nothing except that they would rather be free than safe, and when the cost of freedom came due, you decided it was too high.

The wind fills the sails. The Adamant heels to starboard and the wake stretches out behind you, white on dark water, dissolving. You set a course south. Or east. Or wherever the wind takes you.

Nassau was a place. The sea is a direction.

You choose the direction.

end_pyrrhic.text:
The Adamant is gone.

She burns at the harbour mouth — a column of smoke and fire that rises straight up in the windless morning air. The flames have reached the mainmast now, and it stands like a torch, outlined against the dawn sky. You can hear the fire from the shore — the crack of timbers, the hiss of water turning to steam, the deep structural groaning of a ship that is dying.

You sit on the fort wall with nothing but what you are wearing — a salt-crusted coat, empty pockets, boots full of harbour water. Your cutlass is somewhere on the sea floor. Your pistol is gone. Your ship is burning.

But Ashford's ships are withdrawing.

The Crown's Justice limps toward the horizon, listing badly, her pumps fighting a losing battle against the hole the Dragon's Breath tore in her side. The Resolve has struck her colours. The Perseverance and Fervent are already gone — fled when the explosions started, their captains calculating that a crippled commodore is not worth dying for.

Nassau is free. The blockade is broken. The harbour mouth will be clear by noon, once the Adamant finishes burning.

You watch your ship die. She was a good ship — fast, loyal in the way that ships are loyal, responsive to the helm, forgiving of mistakes. Three years you sailed her. Three years of storms and calms and harbours and the particular partnership between a captain and a vessel that only sailors understand.

She bought Nassau's freedom. It was not a fair trade. Nothing out here is fair.

Delacroix sits down beside you. He has a bottle. He offers it. You drink.

"We won," he says.

"We won," you agree.

The Adamant burns. The harbour opens. Nassau is free, and you have nothing — no ship, no gold, no weapon, nothing but a coat full of salt water and the knowledge that you did what was necessary.

You decide that is enough.

It nearly is.

death_betrayed.text:
You wake in darkness. Your head aches — a deep, throbbing pain at the base of your skull. Your hands are tied behind your back. You are lying on wet stone, and the air smells of mould and low tide.

A cellar. Somewhere near the waterfront, from the sound of water against stone.

The door opens. Light falls across the floor. A man stands in the doorway — the Council's factor, the one who set the ambush. He looks at you without expression.

"I told you it was not personal," he says. "Ashford pays well. Better than Nassau, certainly. Better than whatever you thought you were building here."

Behind him, shadows. More men. The sound of a boat being made ready.

You are taken out before dawn. A fishing boat, running for the blockade. The Crown's Justice receives you at first light — marines on the rail, efficient, professional. They have done this before.

Nassau goes on. The captains will find out in the morning. They will be angry, then practical. Someone will take your place. Someone always takes your place.

Nassau goes on. It does not go on for you.

The charges are read aboard the Crown's Justice. Piracy. Theft of Crown property. Assault on His Majesty's forces. The legal language is meticulous, every word chosen by a man who understands that paperwork outlasts everything.

Your name appears in Crown records as a pirate taken in lawful action. The date is noted. The disposition is noted. The file is closed.

Nassau's harbour is visible through the stern gallery windows. So close. So completely out of reach.

death_ashford.text:
Ashford's marines are thorough.

You are disarmed, searched, and escorted to the brig in under three minutes. The cell is small, clean, and has a lock that a professional locksmith would admire. The marines do not speak. They do not need to — every movement is practiced, efficient, the product of years of training and the particular competence of men who serve under a commodore who tolerates nothing less.

Ashford visits you once. He stands outside the cell in his dress uniform, hands behind his back, and looks at you the way an entomologist looks at a pinned specimen.

"You came aboard my ship," he says. "Under a flag of truce, which I honoured. And you attempted to use stolen Crown documents as leverage against a serving officer of His Majesty's Navy."

He pauses. His voice is measured, precise, utterly without emotion.

"The charge is piracy. The evidence is your presence aboard this vessel, your possession of stolen Crown correspondence, and the testimony of my officers. The sentence, under Admiralty law, is death."

He produces a document — already prepared, already signed. Your name, the date, the charges, the disposition. The paperwork was done before you boarded.

"You will be transferred to Port Royal for trial," he says. "The trial will be brief. The outcome is not in question."

He turns and walks away. His footsteps recede down the corridor. The cell door does not rattle — it is too well-made for that.

You sit on the narrow bench and look at the porthole. Nassau is visible through the thick glass — the harbour, the fort, the town. All of it close enough to see, none of it close enough to reach.

Nassau will not come for you. Nassau does not know where you are, and by the time it finds out, the Crown's Justice will be at sea. Your name will appear in Admiralty records as a pirate taken in lawful action. The file will be closed. The sea will go on.

The light through the porthole dims as the sun sets. Nassau's harbour lanterns begin to glow. They are very beautiful, seen from the inside of a cell on a warship that will carry you to your execution.

They are the last beautiful thing you see.
